planting.note: Plant fresh acorns in fall at 1–2 inches depth immediately upon collection in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, dry to average soil in full sun; Oregon white oak acorns do not require cold stratification and germinate readily. They lose viability quickly and should not be stored dry for long.

directions: Plant fresh acorns in fall in well-drained, dry to average, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Oregon white oak (Garry oak) is the only native oak of the Pacific Northwest north of California, forming open oak savannas and woodlands from British Columbia to California. It is a slow-growing, long-lived, massive tree that can live over 500 years, developing deeply furrowed gray bark and massive, gnarled limbs with age. The oak savannas it creates support extraordinary biodiversity — over 200 species of bees use Oregon white oak, and it supports more invertebrate biodiversity per tree than any other northwest native plant. This ecosystem is now highly threatened, with less than 5% of original oak savanna remaining. The acorns are sweet and palatable, historically processed by indigenous peoples into acorn meal and flour. A landmark native tree for large-scale restoration planting in the Pacific Northwest.

planting.note: Plant fresh acorns in fall at 1–2 inches depth in deep, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, valley or foothill soil in full sun; valley oak requires deep soil to accommodate its extensive taproot. It grows naturally in deep valley soils and does not tolerate shallow, rocky, or waterlogged conditions. Never summer-irrigate established trees.

directions: Plant fresh acorns in fall in deep, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; valley oak is the largest of California's native oaks, reaching 40–100 feet tall with massive, sprawling crowns up to 80 feet wide — widely regarded as one of the most majestic and iconic trees of California. It grows naturally in deep valley soils and oak savanna throughout California's Central Valley and coast ranges. Valley oaks can live over 600 years and develop massive, deeply furrowed, gray trunks and wide-spreading canopies of graceful, deeply-lobed leaves. The large acorns were the primary food staple of numerous California indigenous cultures. CRITICAL: never irrigate established valley oaks in summer — summer water near the root zone encourages Phytophthora root rot (sudden oak death) and kills otherwise healthy trees. Plant away from irrigated lawns and landscapes. A landmark tree requiring ample space but magnificent at maturity.

planting.note: Plant fresh acorns in fall at 1–2 inches depth in well-drained, dry to average, rocky or clay, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; blue oak is highly adapted to the dry, hot summer conditions of California foothill woodlands and is extremely drought-tolerant once established. Do not irrigate in summer after the first year.

directions: Plant fresh acorns in fall in well-drained, dry, rocky to clay, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; blue oak is named for the distinctive bluish or glaucous hue of its deeply lobed, somewhat wavy leaves, which is an adaptation to intense summer sun and drought. It is the dominant oak of the California foothills, forming extensive blue oak woodlands on dry, rocky slopes at 200–3500 feet elevation throughout the Coast Ranges and Sierra Nevada foothills. The tree is semi-deciduous — losing leaves early in summer drought and regaining them with fall rains in addition to the standard spring flush. The relatively sweet acorns were a food staple for numerous California indigenous cultures. Extraordinarily drought and heat tolerant — the blue oak's ability to survive summers with essentially no water after establishment is exceptional among deciduous trees. A landmark species for ecological restoration of California foothill habitats.

planting.note: Plant fresh acorns in fall at 1–2 inches depth in well-drained, dry, sandy, acidic, lean soil in full sun; Arkansas oak is an uncommon red-group oak of dry, sandy pinelands and upland scrub of the lower Mississippi River valley and Gulf Coastal Plain. It is adapted to sandy, infertile, dry, acidic conditions.

directions: Plant fresh acorns in fall in well-drained, dry, sandy, acidic, infertile soil in full sun; Arkansas oak is a relatively uncommon, small-statured oak of the red oak group naturally restricted to dry, sandy, acidic soils in the Gulf Coastal Plain and lower Mississippi River valley, primarily in Arkansas, Louisiana, and Texas. The leaves are variable in shape, sometimes entire, sometimes shallowly lobed, with a distinctive blue-green upper surface from which the common name 'bluejack' derives. A small tree, rarely exceeding 25 feet, it grows naturally in sandy pinelands and upland scrub alongside longleaf pine. The small acorns are eaten by deer and turkeys. An uncommon and undervalued native tree for dry, sandy, acidic sites in the Gulf South where its modest size and adaptability to infertile conditions make it appropriate for naturalistic woodland gardens and pine savanna restoration.

planting.note: Plant fresh acorns in fall at 1 inch depth in well-drained, dry, rocky, calcareous, alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; Mohr's oak is adapted to the thin, rocky limestone soils of the Edwards Plateau and trans-Pecos Texas where it forms extensive colonies by sprouting from deep rhizomes after fire or disturbance.

directions: Plant in dry, rocky, calcareous, alkaline to neutral, thin soil in full sun; Mohr's oak is a small, shrubby, semi-evergreen oak of the limestone hills and rocky grasslands of the Edwards Plateau and west Texas, forming extensive colonies by clonal spreading from rhizomes after fire — making it an important fire-resistant groundcover in Texas hill country landscapes. The small, leathery, pale green leaves are distinctive and remain on the shrub through most of the winter, contributing to the 'evergreen' characteristic of the Edwards Plateau landscape. Drought and heat tolerant in the extreme, naturally growing on the thinnest, rockiest limestone soils with summer rainfall supplementation only. The very small acorns are eaten by scaled quail, wild turkeys, and deer. Valuable for erosion control on dry, rocky, calcareous slopes in the hill country of central Texas.
